---
title: "Social Networks in R"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
---


<center><img src="https://github.com/DACSS-CSSmeths/guidelines/blob/main/pics/small_logo_ccs_meths.jpg?raw=true" width="700"/></center>



------------------------------------------------------------------------


These data frames have been previously prepared, based on the data from [50 influential people in Seattle tech to follow on Twitter](https://www.builtinseattle.com/2018/08/06/50-seattle-tech-twitter-accounts-to-follow). The file can be seen [here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/e/2PACX-1vTpWvtfphnO9eRYwmEyxVAxxo1KEsdpK6sK6q5uhJn5x2QtB-eGiso8ibpF16NaHAers4wDIHkvBo64/pubhtml), and includes 3 sheets:

-   Network as two columns representing edges.
-   Network as adjacency matrix.
-   Attributes of the Network nodes.


# I. Getting Ready:

* Libraries needed

```{r}
# Returns TRUE if installed, FALSE if not
# 1. Define the vector of packages you want to check
packages_to_check <- c("igraph","ggplot2","ggraph","rio")

# 2. Check all of them at once
sapply(packages_to_check, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
```


```{r}
###if needed

# install.packages(c('igraph','ggraph'))
```

* Set seed

```{r}
set.seed(123)
```



# II. Network Creation from dataframes


```{r}
rm(list = ls())
##
fileLink='https://github.com/DACSS-CSSmeths-summer/datafiles/raw/main/seattleTop.xlsx'

edges=rio::import(fileLink, which = 'edges') 
adjacency=rio::import(fileLink, which = 'adjacency')
attributes=rio::import(fileLink,which ="attributes")
```

Use the previous dataframes to create the network:

-   From the edges:

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(igraph) # package needed

EliteNet=graph_from_data_frame(edges, directed = F)

summary(EliteNet)
```

-   From adjacency matrix:

This will rewrit the previous result, here I just show an alternative way:

```{r}
#prepare matrix
row.names(adjacency)=adjacency[,1] #first column for index
adjacency=adjacency[,-1] # deleting the first column
adjacencyMatrix=as.matrix(adjacency) 

# the making...
EliteNet=graph_from_adjacency_matrix(adjacencyMatrix,mode="undirected")


summary(EliteNet)
```
As you saw, you get the same: an undirected graph, with 46 nodes and 393 edges.

See the nodes:

```{r}
V(EliteNet)
```

See the edges:
```{r}
E(EliteNet)
```

## 2.1 Adding Attributes

Attributes are added using **vectors**:


```{r}
# edges
EliteNet=set_edge_attr(EliteNet,"weight", value = 1)

#nodes
EliteNet <- set_vertex_attr(EliteNet, "followers", index = attributes$name, value = attributes$followers)
EliteNet <- set_vertex_attr(EliteNet, "male", index = attributes$name, value = attributes$male)
```

```{r}
summary(EliteNet)
```

You can use attributes to color nodes:

```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(ggraph)

base=ggraph(graph = EliteNet)
base + geom_node_label(aes(label = name,
                           color=as.factor(male)),
                       repel = TRUE,show.legend = F) + 
       geom_edge_link(alpha=0.1) + 
       scale_color_manual(values = c('red','blue'))
  
```

# III. Exploring the Network

Let's some key measures that describe the whole network:

-   Connectedness:

```{r}
is_connected(EliteNet)
```

-   Density: 

```{r}
edge_density(EliteNet)
```

-   Diameter: 

```{r}
diameter(EliteNet)
```

-   Assortativity: 
  a.   Degree assortativity: 

```{r}
assortativity_degree(EliteNet,directed=F)
```


  b. Categorical assortativity: 


```{r}
assortativity_nominal(EliteNet,as.factor(V(EliteNet)$male),directed = F)
```

       
  c. Assortativity (numerical): 


```{r}
assortativity(EliteNet,V(EliteNet)$followers,directed = F)
```

# IV. Exploration of Network nodes

Let's compute measures for each node, and save them into a data frame:


```{r}

eigen=eigen_centrality (EliteNet)$vector

close=closeness(EliteNet,normalized=T)

betw=betweenness(EliteNet,normalized=T)

DFCentrality=as.data.frame(cbind(eigen,close,betw),stringsAsFactors = F)
names(DFCentrality)=c('Eigenvector','Closeness','Betweenness')

DFCentrality$person=row.names(DFCentrality)
row.names(DFCentrality)=NULL

## see
head(DFCentrality)
```

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(DFCentrality, aes(x=Betweenness, y=Closeness)) + 
    theme_classic() +
    geom_text(aes(label=person,size=Eigenvector),show.legend = T,alpha=0.5) 
```

The previous results tells us that two people are salient:

```{r}
HubNodes=dplyr::slice_max(DFCentrality, order_by = Eigenvector, n = 2)$person
HubNodes
```

Let's highlight those guys:

```{r, fig.height=10}
NodeCount=length(V(EliteNet))

V(EliteNet)$label=''

for (index in seq(1:NodeCount)){
    currentName=V(EliteNet)$name[index]
    if (currentName%in%HubNodes){
            V(EliteNet)$label[index]=currentName
    }
}

```

We show here only the hub's names:
```{r, message=F}
library(ggraph)

base=ggraph(graph = EliteNet)
base  + geom_node_label(aes(label = label), 
                        repel = TRUE,
                        show.legend = F,
                        color='red') + 
         geom_edge_link(alpha=0.1)
```

# V. Communities

## 5.1 Potential Communities?

Using algorithm to detect communities is a common practice. But, it is not good to apply them blindly. Then, a basic first step is to check if community emergence is a possibility. 

We can some simulations, without testing statistical significance:

a. A distribution of random network:

```{r}
# Generate an ensemble of 1000 rewired random networks 
set.seed(123)
replicates <- 1000  
random_transitivities <- replicate(replicates, {
  RandomNet <- rewire(EliteNet, 
                      keeping_degseq(niter = gsize(EliteNet) * 10))
  transitivity(RandomNet, type = "global")
})
mean_random_transitivities=mean(random_transitivities)
                                
```

b. The empirical transitivity - our own net 
```{r}
# Calculate your empirical transitivity
empirical_transitivity <- transitivity(EliteNet, type = "global")
```

c. Get Transitivity ratio

```{r}
### Report

cat("EliteNet transitivity:  ", empirical_transitivity, "\n")
cat("Random-network mean:    ", mean_random_transitivities, "\n")
cat("Ratio:                  ", empirical_transitivity / mean(random_transitivities), "\n\n")
```

We should stop if the ratio were 1 or less. As it is not, we may continue:


## 5.2 The algorithms

`igraph` gives you several community-detection algorithms — Louvain, walktrap, fast greedy, infomap, edge betweenness, among others. For this walkthrough we'll narrow it down to two: **Louvain**, since it's the standard modularity-optimizing baseline, and **edge betweenness**, since it takes the opposite approach (cutting bridges instead of merging nodes) and is worth keeping as a point of contrast. The other three are left in the code, commented out, so you can switch them back on and compare for yourself.

```{r, warning=FALSE}
library(igraph)

# Run all five community-detection algorithms
algos <- list(
  louvain          =  { set.seed(123); cluster_louvain(EliteNet) },
  # walktrap         = cluster_walktrap(EliteNet),
  # fast_greedy      = cluster_fast_greedy(EliteNet),
  # infomap          = cluster_infomap(EliteNet),
  edge_betweenness = cluster_edge_betweenness(EliteNet)
)

# Build a summary table: number of clusters, modularity (Q), and cluster sizes
summary_table <- data.frame(
  algorithm   = names(algos),
  n_clusters  = sapply(algos, length),
  modularity_Q  = sapply(algos, modularity),
  cluster_sizes = sapply(algos, function(cl) {
    paste(sort(sizes(cl), decreasing = TRUE), collapse = ", ")
  })
)

# Sort by modularity, descending, so the "best" partition (by this metric) is on top
summary_table <- summary_table[order(-summary_table$modularity_Q), ]
rownames(summary_table) <- NULL

print(summary_table)
```

Louvain finds a moderate partition: 4 communities, modularity ≈ 0.11. That's genuine structure — not zero — but it's also fairly weak by conventional standards (modularity above roughly 0.3 is usually where people start calling a partition "strong"). 

Edge betweenness tells a very different story: 9 clusters, modularity ≈ 0.004 — essentially zero. The resulting partition — one 38-node cluster plus eight singletons, it's the algorithm admitting it couldn't find good bridges to cut in a densely connected network like this one.

Let's continue.

## 5.3 Plotting communities

To actually plot or export these partitions, each node needs its community ID attached as a proper vertex attribute — not just living inside the separate `algos` list objects. We loop over both algorithms and attach each one under its own name (`louvain`, `edge_betweenness`), matched safely by node name rather than position, for the same reason we insisted on `index =` earlier in this document: row order isn't something to trust blindly.

```{r}
for (name in names(algos)) {
  memb <- membership(algos[[name]])
  attr_name <- name
  EliteNet <- set_vertex_attr(EliteNet, attr_name,
                               index = names(memb),
                              # this is important for exporting
                               value = as.vector(memb))
}
# verify new attributes
vertex_attr_names(EliteNet)
```

Now plot the one with the best modularity (Q):

```{r, message=FALSE}
base=ggraph(graph =EliteNet) +
          geom_edge_link(alpha=0.2)

base + geom_node_point(aes(color=as.factor(louvain)), 
                       show.legend = T, size=4)
```

### 5.3.1 Beyond coloring

The plot above colors nodes by Louvain community — but color alone doesn't tell you whether those communities are actually well-separated, or just an assignment overlaid on a network that's fairly interconnected throughout. To check that, we go beyond individual nodes and look at the network at the community level: for each pair of communities (including a community against itself), what fraction of possible connections actually exist? The diagonal shows how densely each community is knit internally; the off-diagonal shows how much "leaks" across to every other community.



```{r}
library(reshape2)

louvain_result <- algos$louvain
memb <- membership(louvain_result)
num_communities <- length(louvain_result)

# comm_edges[i, j] will hold the density between community i and community j
# (diagonal = internal density, off-diagonal = cross-community density)
comm_edges <- matrix(0, nrow = num_communities, ncol = num_communities)

for (i in 1:num_communities) {
  for (j in 1:num_communities) {
    if (i == j) {
      # Internal density of a community -- edge_density() correctly uses
      # 2*edges / (n*(n-1)), no manual formula needed
      sub <- induced_subgraph(EliteNet, which(memb == i))
      comm_edges[i, j] <- edge_density(sub)
    } else {
      # Cross density: edges crossing between community i and community j,
      # divided by every possible pair between the two groups
      nodes_i <- which(memb == i)
      nodes_j <- which(memb == j)
      el <- as_edgelist(EliteNet, names = FALSE)
      boundary <- sum((el[,1] %in% nodes_i & el[,2] %in% nodes_j) |
                       (el[,1] %in% nodes_j & el[,2] %in% nodes_i))
      possible <- length(nodes_i) * length(nodes_j)
      comm_edges[i, j] <- boundary / possible
    }
  }
}

# Plot the density matrix as a heatmap; diagonal cells are internal density,
# off-diagonal cells show how "leaky" the boundary is between each pair
dimnames(comm_edges) <- list(1:num_communities, 1:num_communities)
df <- melt(comm_edges, varnames = c("Community_i", "Community_j"), value.name = "density")

ggplot(df, aes(x = factor(Community_j), y = factor(Community_i), fill = density)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", density)), size = 4) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#FFFFB2", high = "#BD0026", name = "Edge\ndensity") +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(title = "Edge density between communities", x = "Community", y = "Community") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())
```

The table below summarizes the findings from the heatmap.

| Community | Internal | Max external | Ratio    |
| --------- | -------- | ------------ | -------- |
| 1         | 0.505    | 0.333        | **1.51** |
| 2         | 0.562    | 0.360        | **1.56** |
| 3         | 0.582    | 0.382        | **1.52** |
| 4         | 0.900    | 0.382        | **2.36** |

Community 4, the 'strongest,' has internal density 0.90 and max external density 0.38 — a ratio of just over 2:1. Community 1 barely differs from its neighbors at 1.5:1. Real social communities typically show ratios of 5:1 or higher. These are weak, leaky territorial boundaries.


### 5.3.2 Lie with algorithms

A force-directed layout (`fr`, `kk`, and friends) arranges nodes by simulating physical attraction and repulsion — the *position* of any node is partly an accident of that simulation, not a property of the data. A hive layout is different: nodes sit on fixed radial axes, one axis per community, ordered by a real attribute (here, degree). Nothing about *where* a node lands is random.

But "not random" isn't the same as "trustworthy." A hive layout will draw clean, confidently separated spokes for *any* partition you feed it — a strong one or a weak one, real structure or algorithmic noise. It can't tell the difference, and it won't warn you either way. That's the trap this section title is about: the layout doesn't lie by fabricating anything, it lies by *omission* — by rendering every partition, however weak, with the same visual confidence.


```{r, fig.width=10}
V(EliteNet)$degree = degree(EliteNet) # first compute node degree and assign
ggraph(EliteNet, layout = 'hive', 
       axis = louvain,
       sort.by = degree
       ) +   
  geom_edge_hive(color='grey80') +   
  geom_axis_hive(aes(colour = as.factor(louvain)), 
                 label = F) + 
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = as.factor(louvain))) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), 
                 repel = TRUE, 
                 max.overlaps = 50, # avoid overlap
                 size = 2.5) +
  coord_fixed()
```

### 5.4 Was any of this real?

The ratio table and heatmap were the first crack in the story: internal density was higher than external, but well under the 5:1 benchmark for real social communities. That's what started the doubt, not confirmed anything.

The hive plot pushed the doubt further, from a different angle: it showed that a layout will draw confident, cleanly separated axes for *any* partition you hand it, real structure or not. Visually forcing communities onto radial spokes doesn't mean those communities earned their separation — it means hive layouts always look like this.

So we close by asking the one question neither the ratio table nor the hive plot can answer on their own: is EliteNet's clustering actually higher than what a degree-matched random network would produce, or is the "community" story we've been telling just chance dressed up in color?


```{r}
null_mean <- mean(random_transitivities)
null_sd   <- sd(random_transitivities)
z_score   <- (empirical_transitivity - null_mean) / null_sd
count_exceeding <- sum(random_transitivities >= empirical_transitivity)
p_value <- count_exceeding / replicates   
decision_05 <- if (p_value < 0.05) "SIGNIFICANT" else "NOT significant"
decision_01 <- if (p_value < 0.01) "SIGNIFICANT" else "NOT significant"
cat("z-score:                 ", z_score, "\n")
cat("Empirical p-value:       ", p_value, paste0("(", count_exceeding, "/", replicates, ")"), "\n")
cat("alpha = 0.05 -->", decision_05, "\n")
cat("alpha = 0.01 -->", decision_01, "\n")
```

The result: z = 1.33, p = 0.094 — not significant at either threshold. EliteNet's clustering is not distinguishable from what its degree sequence alone would produce by chance.

This is the punchline for the whole section: the ratio table planted the first doubt, the hive plot showed how easily a visualization can paper over that doubt with confident colors and clean spokes, and the formal test confirms the doubt was right all along. That gap — between what a plot *suggests* and what a null model actually *supports* — is the habit worth keeping: always ask what a visualization would look like even if the pattern were pure noise, before trusting that it isn't.

Time to export:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
# so this works
write_graph(EliteNet, "EliteNet_R.graphml", "graphml")
```
